title: How to BLOW UP Your Channel WITHOUT Making Videos

transcript.text: So, this is almost too incredible to believe, but in the last 28 days, our community posts have pulled in over 400,000 views. Now, not a single one of these is a new video, and most of them took no more than 2 minutes to write. So, that's more views, less time, and a lot more opportunities to grow your channel faster. And that is just one of seven juicy strategies sitting inside YouTube right now that most creators are completely ignoring. Some are inside the YouTube Studio itself. One of them works on content you made years ago. And one will open up your channel to audiences who've never been able to watch you before at all. The first strategy on this list requires zero new content. So, what I want you to do is take a look at this graph right here. It's a real video's audience retention graph. And the first thing you might want to notice is that big drop right there at the very beginning. Now, look at what happens after that initial 30 seconds. See how that line flattens out almost completely? For the rest of the video, nearly 7 minutes, almost nobody's left. words, who made it past the intro watched pretty much the whole thing. What it tells me is that this isn't a bad video. It's a great video, actually, but it does have a bad intro. >> [music] >> Because buried behind those first 30 seconds is content that people genuinely want to watch and are satisfied by. They just never got around to actually watching it. YouTube's algorithm judges the health of your content based on how people behave when they watch it. That early and very steep cliff, all of those people bailing after the first 30 seconds registers a pretty major red flag. Now, most creators look at a graph like this and think to themselves, "Noted. I'll do better in the next video." And they move on and leave that video to die a slow and quiet death. But you don't have to do that, because YouTube actually gives you a tool to go back into a published video and fix it. So, I'm in the desktop YouTube Studio on the content screen, and I'm going to click on that video that we were looking at earlier. And then go into the editor from the menu bar down the left-hand side. Now, up here at the top, you'll see an option called trim and cut. Click that. What this lets you do is drag along the timeline and remove entire sections of your video, even after it's already been published and it's live to viewers. So, that slow intro at the beginning of a video where we spend a minute explaining [music] what we're about to explain before we actually explain it, gone. Instead, what we're going to do is start the video at this natural breakpoint where the audience retention really levels out. Let's get this out of the way, shall we? Lots of people love the idea of being a YouTuber. So, in this example, all we need to do is simply drag and shorten the video, missing out that entire first minute of video abandonment, and click save. Let's get this out of the way, shall we? Lots of people Now, YouTube will take an hour or two to process the changes, but when it does, that retention graph is likely to start recovering. The key principle is this, you cannot add new footage to a published video, but you absolutely can remove the parts that are killing that video. And this flatline in the audience retention graph that we saw earlier, that's your proof it's worth doing. All right then, on to the next one. How many of you are actually using community posts on your channel? Well, here's something you might not be considering because you don't even know it's happening. Community posts are no longer just appearing on the home feed as you scroll for something to watch. YouTube has now started surfacing community posts directly into the YouTube Shorts feed. Yeah, let me just say that again for dramatic effect. >> The Shorts feed. >> That is the same feed that gets hundreds of billions of views per day. And here is what it looks like in practice. You're scrolling through the Shorts feed, and instead of a video, you get an image with text where the title should be, appearing natively in the feed in front of people who have never seen your channel before. And you don't get to decide which of these community posts turn into Shorts, YouTube just does it for you. Which is a bit weird. This is the VidIQ channel broken down by content type in the past 28 days. Shorts are at the top with 2 and 1/2 million views, videos a solid million views, and then look at the bottom two. Yeah, live streams with 28,000 views, and then this brand new content type, posts, over 400,000 views. So, with some quick maths, I think community posts got 14 times more views than our live streams. Same breakdown now, sorted by new viewers. Now, when we look at these metrics, posts brought in almost 10,000 brand new viewers. Live streams brought in a modest 2,200. So, simply put, more than four times as many people discover vidIQ from community posts that take 2 minutes to write over live streams that take hours to prepare and shoot. This is the opportunity sitting on your community tab right now, completely untouched. So, just as a reminder, it is community post images >> [music] >> that get turned into shorts, and you have to respect the twitchy finger of a swiping shorts viewer. So, keep these images simple. Think of them like memes. You as a viewer should be able to get the idea in 3 to 5 seconds. That doesn't mean they have to be funny, like memes, but they just need to respect the idea of an instant connection with a viewer, or they will swipe onto the next short. So, then, community posts are quietly pulling in new viewers while you sleep. But, here's the problem. A viewer [music] is not a subscriber, not yet anyway. And getting someone to your channel is only half the battle. You still need to get them to actually hit that button. So, what I want you to do is open up a new tab and go to your YouTube channel home page. Look at the URL in the address bar, and it looks something like this. Now, here's where things get a little interesting. We're going to add a tiny piece of code to the end of that URL. Just type this exactly as you see it on screen now. Now, watch what happens when I refresh the page. It triggers an instant subscribe pop-up when the page reloads. So, instead of viewers landing on your channel page and maybe remembering to subscribe at some point, they're immediately prompted to do so. There's a psychology concept called choice architecture. People are far more likely to take action where the next step is obvious and frictionless, and it's right in front [music] of them. In reality, most viewers don't avoid subscribing to our channel because they hate your content. They just never get prompted at the right moment, and this link fixes that moment. Now, from an analytical point of view, after the video watch page and short player, the channel page is the third biggest source of subscribers for us. So, it's worth tweaking these links to make sure that source is even more lucrative for new subscribers. So, where do you use it? Everywhere you already have an audience. Anywhere you'd normally drop a generic channel link, swap it for this one instead, and every person who clicks on it is now being given a clear, frictionless reason to subscribe the moment they arrive. So, now you've got a link that's converting visitors into subscribers automatically when they come to your channel page. But, here is the next question. What about every other place on YouTube where they come into contact with you and have the opportunity to subscribe? So, I'm in the YouTube studio, and I'm going to click on analytics and then go to the content tab at the top and scroll down, >> [music] >> and you'll see a section called how viewers find you, which is basically the traffic sources of your views. So, this tells you at a glance exactly how people are finding your videos. What I want you to do at this point is think about the difference in mindset between what I call intent versus serendipity. For example, take the intent of somebody who's just searched for best camera for travel in 2026. They have a specific problem, and if your video solves it, that subscribe button becomes their way of saying, "I trust this person to solve my future problems, too." For a channel like vidIQ, search drives over 50% of our traffic because we focus on solving specific problems. If your search-to-subscriber ratio is high, you've just found your authority-based growth engine. Now, here's where things can get really interesting. If you make content in multiple formats, you can drill it deeper into the details. So, back on the analytics screen here, I'm going to click on videos, and again, look at the traffic sources. Look at how these numbers have changed significantly. Now, browse is our strongest traffic source from long-form videos only. Shorts is where we clearly get more of our search traffic views. Now, why does any of this matter for growing your subscribers without making new videos? Well, once you know which traffic sources convert best on your channel, you can start pointing your existing content to those traffic sources. If search is your highest converting source, go back to your published videos and optimize the titles and the descriptions with the exact language people are actually searching for. And if a traffic source is sending you lots of views, but almost no subscribers, that's not necessarily a failure, but it is a signal. It means those viewers aren't connecting deeply enough with your content to want more, which is likely a packaging problem, not necessarily a content problem. And now I want to go into an even deeper layer, because within your own content, some of your videos are absolute subscriber magnets, and you probably have no idea which ones those are. So, this is vidIQ's content sorted by views, and on the surface, it looks pretty healthy. How to get more YouTube Shorts has almost 220,000 views, and another few videos have over 100,000 views. But watch now when I change one single thing. I'm going to now sort this exact table by subscribers gained [music] instead of views. And as you can see, the list completely reorganizes itself. Our top video by views doesn't even appear in the top five anymore. It's way down in the list here. But look at this one about demonetization and inauthentic content. Double the number of subscribers gained with a quarter of the views, 55,000 of them. This is what a subscriber to view ratio audit reveals. You have videos sitting quietly on your channel right now that are converting viewers into subscribers at an extraordinary rate. But, because the view count on those videos isn't flashy, nobody's [music] paying attention to it. Here's exactly what to do with this information, and I'm going to boil it down into three actionable steps. First, study it. Why did this particular video convert so well? What was the topic? Who was it speaking to? In our case, it's typically someone who's just installed vidIQ and is already invested in their YouTube journey. Second, promote it. This is a video that goes in your end screen. This is a video you link in your newsletter. If this video converts strangers into subscribers better than anything else you've made, then more people need to find it. And the third thing you can do is replicate it. Build your next five video ideas around these same format, same audience moment, same type of pain point. You now have a data-backed content strategy, not from guesswork, but from your own analytics. And to find yours, go to the YouTube studio, click analytics, then select advanced mode in the top right-hand corner, and then on this table, sort the data by the subscriber column. For this specific research activity, you're looking past the view counts. You're finding the videos that quietly convert at a rate nothing else can touch. That is your blueprint, and it's been sitting there the whole time. So, now you know which videos are our best subscriber converters. But, what about the videos that should be converting? Videos where people are watching almost all the way through, but nobody's clicking on them in the first place. Average view duration is one of the strongest signals of content quality on YouTube. If people are watching 80%, 65%, or more realistically 50% of your video, that means they liked it. But, here is a scenario that happens more often than you think. A video has great watch time, great average view duration, the people who watch it love it, but the views are disappointingly low. The problem isn't what's inside the video. The problem is the door Nobody's clicking in. Here's how to find these videos on your channel. Go to the YouTube studio, click analytics, then click the content tab, and finally click the videos button because this type of research doesn't really work with shorts. Next, click the see more button here, and finally, switch the breakdown from content type to popular content. Now, what you're looking for here in this table are videos where average view duration is high, but impressions to click-through rate is relatively low. Anything around or under 3% on established videos is probably worth flagging. And that gap between those two numbers is the opportunity, repackaging. A high average view duration tells you that the content is good. A low click-through rate tells you that the title and the thumbnail simply aren't doing their job. And the title and the thumbnail are the only things that you can really change at this point. Think about what that means practically. You've already done the research, you've already filmed it, and edited it, and uploaded it. The hard work is well and truly done. The content is proven. It's just not being given the chance to reach the audience it deserves. So, when you're coming up with a new title to repackage this video, ask yourself, does this title make someone feel like they're missing out? Does it speak directly to a specific problem or outcome? Is it clear in under 3 seconds what this video is going to do for me, the viewer? And for that thumbnail, is there a clear focal point? Is there contrast? Does it create curiosity or communicate a benefit immediately? Would you click it if you saw it while you were scrolling through the feed? Now, so far, everything we've covered has been about working smarter with what you've already made. But this next one is different because it takes content you've already made and multiplies it without you changing a single word, filming a single frame, or spending a single extra hour in the editing suite. YouTube has a feature called auto dubbing, and what it does is automatically translate and dub your existing videos into other languages. record button. Not subtitles, an actual dubbed audio track in another language synced to your video, served automatically to viewers in those markets. Which means videos you made months or even years ago, possibly decades ago, are suddenly accessible to audiences you never deliberately targeted without you changing a single thing. Look at our own analytics. Nearly 20% of our views, over 800,000 of them every single month, are now coming from India alone. By enabling auto dubbing for languages like Hindi, we are able to reach those viewers, and we can speak to them in their native tongue, which significantly increases the chance they'll watch more of our content and ultimately subscribe to us. So, let me show you how to turn it on. Since you're already logged into the studio, click settings in the left menu bar, then channel, and go to advanced settings. Scroll down and check the box that says allow automatic dubbing. It's as simple as that. Now, admittedly, I do have quite a big disclaimer for this one. Is the dubbing perfect? No. The voice isn't yours. Occasionally, the sync isn't flawless, either. But, viewers in those markets are not comparing it to a native speaker. They're comparing it to nothing because before this feature existed, they simply couldn't access your content at all. And the reality is, like all AI at the moment, this tool is going to get better and better, and critically, it is free. And if it doesn't work, your viewers hate it, and it has a negative impact on your video numbers, it's really easy to turn off the whole thing again if you need to. What you have there are seven ways to grow your subscribers without making a single new video. But, to really get these strategies to work even better, you need to truly understand how the YouTube algorithm is making its decisions in this day and age. And in this video right here, we break down exactly how the algorithm works at every level. Beginner, intermediate, advanced, it doesn't matter where you are in your journey. There is something in this one that will change how you think about every video you make from this point forward.
